coredata.dc:title: A facile fluorescence platform for chromium and ascorbic acid detection based on “on-off-on” strategy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Yanchun
Wu, Suling
Duan, Zhengyi
Song, Shengmei
Shuang, Shaomin
Gong, Xiaojuan
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a facile and rapid fluorescence “on–off-on” strategy for the detection of chromium (Cr(VI)) and ascorbic acid (AA) was developed, which was based on the water-soluble carbon dots (CDs). The CDs was synthesized by a microwave-assisted treatment of L-tartaric acid, citric acid, and urea. The CDs have many advantages, such as high fluorescence quantum yield (20.5%) and good fluorescence stability. Based on inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching, the fluorescence of the CDs can be quenched by Cr(VI) quickly; while the reduction of IFE and reducing action can make the fluorescence of the CDs recover by AA efficiently. Moreover, under the optimal experimental conditions, the CDs had a good detection performance for Cr(VI) in the range of 0.8 ∼ 189 µM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.16 µM. The linear detection for AA was ranged from 0.43 to 25.7 µM with a LOD of 0.1 µM. More importantly, the as-constructed fluorescence detecting platform was successfully applied for Cr(VI) and AA detection in the environmental samples and fruit samples, respectively. In addition, the application potential of the CDs in fluorescent films and anti-counterfeiting materials was further discussed in detail. This work will provide a novel idea for designing a portable sensor based on the CDs to quickly and sensitively detect Cr(VI) and AA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Chromium (VI)
Ascorbic acid
Fluorescence platform
“on–off-on” strategy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The detection platform for Cr(VI) and AA
The application potential of the CDs in other areas
Detection of Cr(VI) and AA based on the CDs
Optimizing conditions for Cr(VI) and AA detection
Sensing mechanism for Cr(VI) and AA
The application potential of the CDs in other areas

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Typical TEM image of the CDs. Inset: the HRTEM image. (b) Histogram of the size distribution of the CDs obtained from the representative TEM image.
(a) FTIR, (b) XPS survey scan, (c) C 1s, and (d) N 1s spectra of the CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of the CDs. Insets: the photographs of the CDs under visible light (left) and UV light (right). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 320 to 500 nm.
(a) Fluorescence intensities of the different concentrations of the CDs. (b) Relative fluorescence intensity of the CDs, CDs + Cr(VI), and CDs + Cr(VI) + AA in different pHs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs with the different concentrations of Cr(VI) (0 ∼ 300 μM). Inset: the linear plot of F0/F and the concentration of Cr(VI) (0.88 ∼ 189.33 μM). (b) Relative fluorescence intensity of the CDs with different metal ions and anions with a concentration of 1.0 mM at 467 nm. (c) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs/Cr(VI) system with the different concentrations of AA (0 ∼ 270 μM). Inset: the linear plot of F-F0 and the concentration of AA (0.43 ∼ 25.74 μM). (d) Relative fluorescence intensity of the CDs/Cr(VI) system with the interfering substances with a concentration of 0.15 mM at 467 nm.

(a) The overlap between the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) and excitation and emission spectra of the CDs. (b) Fluorescence decay of the CDs (0.125 mg/mL) in the absence and presence of different concentration of Cr(VI) as a function of time at excitation/emission wavelengths of 405/467 nm.

(a) Left: photographs of the CDs under daylight lamp (upper) and UV light (below). Right: photographs of the CDs under daylight lamp (upper) and UV light (below) (“S”: CDs; “X”: CDs + Cr(VI); “U”: CDs + Cr(VI) + AA). (b) Photographs of the CDs and the CDs + Cr(VI) under daylight lamp (left) and UV light (right).
Illustration for the synthetic procedure of the CDs by the microwave-assisted method and the “on–off-on” fluorescence detecting platform for Cr(VI) and AA based on the CDs.
Detection results of Cr(VI) in different actual samples (n = 5).
Detection results of AA in fruit samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A facile fluorescence platform for chromium and ascorbic acid detection based on “on-off-on” strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a facile and rapid fluorescence “on–off-on” strategy for the detection of chromium (Cr(VI)) and ascorbic acid (AA) was developed, which was based on the water-soluble carbon dots (CDs). The CDs was synthesized by a microwave-assisted treatment of L-tartaric acid, citric acid, and urea. The CDs have many advantages, such as high fluorescence quantum yield (20.5%) and good fluorescence stability. Based on inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching, the fluorescence of the CDs can be quenched by Cr(VI) quickly; while the reduction of IFE and reducing action can make the fluorescence of the CDs recover by AA efficiently. Moreover, under the optimal experimental conditions, the CDs had a good detection performance for Cr(VI) in the range of 0.8 ∼ 189 µM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.16 µM. The linear detection for AA was ranged from 0.43 to 25.7 µM with a LOD of 0.1 µM. More importantly, the as-constructed fluorescence detecting platform was successfully applied for Cr(VI) and AA detection in the environmental samples and fruit samples, respectively. In addition, the application potential of the CDs in fluorescent films and anti-counterfeiting materials was further discussed in detail. This work will provide a novel idea for designing a portable sensor based on the CDs to quickly and sensitively detect Cr(VI) and AA.

ce:section.ce:para: With rapidly growing of urbanization and industrialization,hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI))pollution in the environment has become increasinglyserious. Cr(VI) is a kind of heavy metal pollutant with strong oxidative toxicity, and even low doses or concentrations of Cr(VI) can cause carcinogenesis and mutagenesis. After being discharged into water bodies and soil, Cr(VI) will further accumulate through the food chain and bring great threats to the safety of the ecosystem. In order to avoid the harm of Cr(VI) in the ecosystem to humans, many countries and organizations have laid down strict restrictions on the discharge of Cr(VI) contaminants into the environment and the allowable concentration of Cr(VI) in the environment. For example, a limit of Cr(VI) concentration in drinking water (<0.05 mg/L, 0.96 µM) has been set by the World Health Organization (WHO) [1]. The maximum contaminant level for total Cr(VI) in drinking water should be lower than 0.1 mg/L (1.92 µM), which has been set by US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [2]. Consequently, it is critical to develop a fast and accurate method for Cr(VI) detection. Various techniques have been developed to detect Cr(VI), including chromatography [3,4], spectrophotometry [5], atomic absorption spectrometry [6], electroanalytical [7], flow injection analyses [8], X-ray absorption spectroscopy [9], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [10], and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [11]. Nevertheless, these methods inevitably have some defects, such as expensive instrumentation, tedious pretreatment, and possible secondary contamination from the reagents, limiting their large-scale application. Therefore, it is one of the high priority research directions to develop low-cost, convenient, fast, and green method for Cr(VI) detection.

Ascorbic acid (AA), also known as vitamin C, is an essential bioactive molecule, acting as a powerful antioxidant. Being an essential and exogenous vitamin, AA can only be obtained through the diet in our daily life and performs diverse physiological functions in humans, involving biosynthesis and metabolism. AA deficiency can bring about scurvy and hypoimmunity, while excess AA can cause stomach acid, diarrhea, coronary heart disease, and other diseases [12]. Therefore, it is essential to detect the content of AA in body fluid or food, cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, and other products. So far, many methods have been developed to detect AA, including titration with an oxidizing agent [13], spectrophotometry [14], electrochemical method [15], chromatography [16], chemiluminescence [17], enzymology [18], capillary electrophoreses [19], etc. Nevertheless, these methods cannot meet the requirements of actual sample testing, so we hope to find a simple, convenient, and effective method to detect the content of AA.

Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging star in the intersection of traditional quantum dots (TQDs) and carbon materials, have drawn extensive attention and interest from researchers in recent years. Compared with TQDs, the CDs own unique advantages, such as good water solubility, photobleaching resistance, enhanced chemical stability, biocompatibility, and environmental friendliness, which lay the foundation for its extensive application in various fields, including bio-imaging [20], medicine [21,22], detection [23,24], photocatalysis [25], anti-counterfeiting [26], optoelectronic devices and energy storage [27–29], etc. Thereinto, fluorescence detection is one of the most important applications for the CDs, attributing to many sensors (pH [30,31], different ions [32,33], and small molecules [34–36]) based on the CDs have the advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity, and fast detection. In particular, the green and sustainable “on-off-on” strategy based on the CDs has more advantages [37,38], such as flexible design, easy to achieve multi-target simultaneous detection, strong anti-interference ability, etc., which has been successfully used to detect ions and molecules [35,36,39]. Therefore, it is easy to design an “on-off-on” fluorescence detecting platform based on the CDs for realizing the sensitive detection of Cr(VI) and AA.

Herein, we developed a simple microwave-assisted synthesis route to prepare fluorescent CDs with a quantum yield of 20.5%, using L-tartaric acid, citric acid, and urea as the reaction precursors. As shown in Scheme 1 , the apparent fluorescence quenching occurred with the addition of Cr(VI) to the CDs, and then the significant fluorescence recovery can be observed after AA being added into the CDs/Cr(VI) system. According to this phenomenon, a novel “on-off-on” fluorescence detecting platformforCr(VI) and AA was constructed. Compared with other reported strategies, the presented method is more straightforward and faster with milder condition (room temperature in ultrapure water) and higher sensitivity (KSV = 2.244 × 104 L/mol). Moreover, this platform has been used as fluorescent ink without and with Cr(VI) or AA for drawing luminescent patterns, showing favorable application in anticounterfeit and the detection of Cr(VI) and AA in test strips.

Citric acid (CA), L-tartaric acid (TA), and urea were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagents Company (Tianjin, China). Quinine sulfate was obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Other reagents were from Beijing Chemical Reagents Company (Beijing, China). All reagents are analytical reagent grade or above. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ·cm) was purified through a Molecular purification system (Shanghai, China), which was used throughout the experiments.

A green and simple microwave pyrolysis synthetic route was adopted. The details synthesis steps are as follows: different proportions of L-tartaric acid, citric acid, and urea were completely dissolved in a 100 mL beaker with 15 mL ultrapure water. The beaker was placed into the microwave oven to handle the mixture for 8, 10, or 12 min under 300 W, respectively. After the reaction was completed, the obtained brownish-black solid was dispersed into 15 mL ultrapure water, and then the solution was successively filtered through a Φ 9 cm filter paper and a 0.22 µm (Φ 13 mm) microporous filtration membrane. A clear and brownish aqueous solution was obtained and lyophilized to yield the dry product, which was stored for the following experiments.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of the CDs was acquired by an FEI Tecnai G2F20 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) with an operating voltage of 200 kV. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the CDs was obtained by a Bruker Tensor II Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bremen, Germany) and a Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. The elemental analysis of the CDs was achieved by an Elementar Vario Micro Cube (Germany). The UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, and fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs were gained by a Lambda 365 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Llantrisant, U.K.), a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian Cary Eclipse, U.K.), and an FLS 920 time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., England), respectively. Different pH values of the solution were adjusted by an FE20 pH meter (Mettler Toledo, Switzerland).

The relative quantum yield（QY）of the as-prepared CDs was determined by reference to quinine sulfate [35], which was dissolved in 0.10 M H2SO4 (QY = 54% at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm with the refractive index (η) of 1.33). The CDs was dissolved in ultrapure water. The QY of the CDs was calculated as the following equation:
Q Y
S
= Q Y
R
I S
A S
A R
I R
η
S
2
η
R
2

Herein, the subscripts “S” and “R” represent the sample and the reference, respectively. “I” and “A” refer to the fluorescence integral area and UV–vis absorbance at an excitation wavelength of 357 nm, respectively. “η” is the refractive index of the solutions, and the refractive index of both the sample and the reference is 1.33. Among them, it should be noted that the UV absorbance value (A) is kept below 0.1 in order to minimize the reabsorption effects of the solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection platform for Cr(VI) and AA
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the selectivity of the detection platform for Cr(VI), 20 μL of twenty metal ions and fourteen anions (0.1 M) were added into 2.0 mL CDs solution (0.125 mg/mL), respectively, including K+, Na+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cr2O7 2-, F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, ClO4 -, HSO3 -, H2PO4 -, NO3 –, SCN-, C2O4 2-, HPO4 2-, SO4 2-, CO3 2–, and S2O3 2-, and then the fluorescence intensities were recorded. The interference experiments were carried out in detail. 0.2 mL metal ions (0.1 M) or anions (0.1 M) were added into 1.8 mL CDs solution (0.125 mg/mL) containing 10 μL Cr(VI) (0.1 M), respectively, and then the fluorescence intensities were recorded. After that, the titration experiment of the sensitivity for Cr(VI) detection was performed. A certain amount of Cr(VI) (0.1 M) was gradually dropped into a cuvette filled with 2.0 mL of CDs solution (0.125 mg/mL) using a micro-syringe, and then the fluorescence intensities at the emission wavelength of 467 nm were recorded.

The detection of AA was based on the CDs/Cr(VI) system, consisting of a 1990 μL of CDs solution (0.125 mg/mL) and a 10 μL of Cr(VI) (0.1 M). To evaluate the selectivity of the CDs/Cr(VI) system for the detection of AA, 30 μL of various biological and environment-related substances (0.01 M) were added into the system, respectively, including alanine (Ala), asparticacid (Asp), tryptophan (Trp), threonine (Thr), isoleucine (Iso), serine (Ser), histidine (His), asparagine (Asn), lysine (Lys), glycine (Gly), leucine (Leu), glutamine (Glu), cysteine (Cys), tyrosine (Tyr), dopamine (DA), and H2O2, and then the fluorescence intensities were recorded. Next, the interference experiments were carried out. 20 μL interfering substances mentioned above were added into the CDs/Cr(VI)/AA system to measure the fluorescence intensities, respectively. After that, the titration experiment of AA was also conducted to demonstrate the sensitivity for the detection of AA. A givenvolumeof AA (0.1 M) was stepwise added into the CDs/Cr(VI) system using a micro-syringe to shake for 3 min, and then the fluorescence intensities were measured. All fluorescence intensities were recorded at the excitation and emission wavelengths of 357 nm and 467 nm with the slit width of 5 nm, respectively. The detection of Cr(VI) and AA were carried out at room temperature and repeated three times.

The actual water samples were collected from tap water (Taiyuan, China) and Fenhe river water (Taiyuan, China) to detect Cr(VI). The samples were treated by the following pre-treatment steps to remove some possible impurities, ensuring that the samples remain pure. These water samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min and then filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous filtration membrane (pH 7.0) to spike with different concentrations of Cr(VI). Soil samples were collected from Lvliang Mountain, Shanxi Province, which was treated according to the literature to obtain the corresponding soil sample solution for detecting Cr(VI) [40]. The pH of the obtained soil sample solution was adjusted to 7.0 by adding NaOH solution.

Fresh fruit samples (orange, red pitaya, and kiwi) were purchased from a local market to detect AA after pre-treatment. First, all samples were washed and dried, and then processed into powder in a commercial blender. After that, they were centrifugated at 8000 rpm for 10 min in a refrigerated centrifuge (10 °C), and then the supernatant was left as the sample stock solution for further use. An appropriate sample stock solution was added to PBS solution (pH 7.0) to detect and spike with AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application potential of the CDs in other areas
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs was applied in the preparation of the fluorescent film. 5.0 g of PVA was placed in 50 mL ultrapure water, which was heated at 95 °C and stirred rapidly until it was clear. After that, the PVA solution was mixed with the different contents of the CDs. Then, the obtained mixture solutions were injected into the molds and dried at 60 °C for 120 min. And the fluorescent flexible films were obtained. We also used the fluorescent film to verify the “off–on” effect of the CDs by adding Cr(VI) or AA, respectively.

The CDs was applied in information security and anti-counterfeiting. The solution obtained above was smeared on the “Yang” of a piece of filter paper painted with Tai Chi diagram. Meanwhile, the solution with Cr(VI) was smeared on the “Yin” of the filter paper.

The synthetic method can be used to prepare different types of the CDs by tuning the mass ratio of reaction precursors and reaction time as shown in Fig. S1. When the mass ratio of the three precursors (L-tartaric acid, citric acid, and urea) is constant, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the prepared CDs increases gradually with the extension of time, and then that decreases when the time is longer than 10 min. Therefore, 10 min is considered as the optimal reaction time. For the reaction precursors, when the ratio of the three precursors (1.5 g of L-tartaric acid, 1.5 g of citric acid, and 1.5 g of urea) is 1:1:1, the CDs have the highest fluorescence QY, which serves as the optimum proportion of reaction precursors for the synthesis of the CDs. The QY of the as-prepared CDs was determined by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensities and absorbance of the CDs with the reference of quinine sulfate (Fig. S2a), which was calculated as 20.5% (Fig. S2b).

The morphology and the microstructure of the as-prepared CDs were obtained from transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fig. 1 a), showing that the CDs are approximately quasi-spherical and uniformly distributed on the copper grid. The distinct lattice fringes with a lattice parameter of 0.21 nm can be observed by HRTEM image (the inset in Fig. 1a), which is corresponded to the in-plane lattice fringe (100) facet of graphene [41,42]. A relevant histogram is shown in Fig. 1b, depicting that a narrow diameter distribution of the CDs is about 1.75–3.75 nm with an average size of 2.6 nm according to the Gauss Model.

The composition and surface functional groups of the as-prepared CDs were identified by FTIR and XPS. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the peaks at about 3436 cm−1 and 3200 cm−1 are ascribed to the N-H and O-H stretching vibrations, respectively [22,43,44]. The peaks at 2770 cm−1 and 1715 cm−1 are attributed to C-H stretching vibration and CO stretching vibration, respectively [22,43,44]. The CC stretching vibrations or the existence of NCO may be assigned to the peak at 1612 cm−1 [22,44]. The sharp peak at 1401 cm−1 is manifested the existence of aromatic CN stretching vibration [22,42,44]. Two peaks at 1190 cm−1 and 1053 cm−1 are indicated the existence of CO stretching vibration [45,46]. It can be seen from Fig. 2b, there are three peaks at 285.0 eV, 397.0 eV, and 529.0 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The results of the elemental analysis shown in Table S1 indicate that the contents of C, H, N, and O (calculated) of the CDs are 34.59%, 5.32%, 14.84%, and 45.25%, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 2c, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum can be disassembled into four peaks at 285.1 eV, 286.5 eV, 288.4 eV, and 289.0 eV, implying four types of carbon bonds C-N, CO, CN, and CO, respectively [42,43]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) is decomposed into three peaks at 399.6 eV, 400.4 eV, and 401.5 eV, corresponding to CNC, NC3, and NH, respectively. The O 1s spectrum in Fig. S3 can be dissociated into two peaks of COH and CO at 531.7 eV and 532.6 eV, respectively [42,45,47]. All the results demonstrate that some hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl can be found on the surface of the CDs, which are not only closely related to good solubility and stability ofthe CDs, but also are conducive to the formation of different surface defects and the generation of energy gaps on the CDs to ensure effectively photoinduced luminescence [46].

The optical properties of the CDs were explored by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, two absorption peaks are centered at 217 nm and 376 nm, which are ascribed to the π → π* transition of the conjugated CC bond of the aromatic sp2 domains and the n → π* transition of C=O and C=N of the CDs, respectively [45]. When the CDs was excited at 357 nm, the optimal emission peak was at 467 nm. The insert images are the photographs of the CDs solution in cuvette under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). Besides, the fluorescence behavior of the CDs under different excitation wavelengths is illustrated in Fig. 3b. When the excitation wavelength varies from 320 nm to 400 nm, no shift of the emission wavelength is observed. Whereas, the emission wavelength is redshifted from 525 nm to 585 nm with the excitation wavelength changing from 420 nm to 520 nm. This excitation-dependence fluorescence behavior of the CDs may be attributed to the different surface states of the CDs. More importantly, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs does not change under continuous Xenon lamp irradiation for 90 min (Fig. S4a), showing the CDs have excellent resistance to photobleaching. As described in Fig. S4b, excellent salt-tolerance of the CDs was verified in different concentrations of NaCl solutions. Moreover, no obvious changes can be found in the CDs solution after that was stored for six months. Therefore, the stability of the CDs ensures the potential practical applications value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) and AA based on the CDs
Optimizing conditions for Cr(VI) and AA detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to establish a good and stable platform for the detection of Cr(VI) and AA, many detection conditions including concentration, pH, and reaction time should be investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced gradually with the increase of the CDs concentration, and then it tended to be stable when the concentration of the CDs was higher than 0.125 mg/mL, so 0.125 mg/mL was regarded as a favorable concentration of the CDs. And we also noticed that the concentration of Cr(VI) directly affected the detection range of the CDs/Cr(VI) system to AA. A low concentration of Cr(VI) cannot wholly quench the fluorescence of the CDs, resulting in a narrow linear range for AA detection. In comparison, a high concentration of Cr(VI) was used in the detection system, AA can first react with free Cr(VI) in the solution instead of reacting with the CDs/Cr(VI) compound, resulting in a low sensitivity for AA detection. So, 189.33 μM was selected as the optimum Cr(VI) concentration in the CDs/Cr(VI) system for the detection of AA. The influence of pH values on the detection platform is described in Fig. 4b. Specifically, the fluorescent intensity of the CDs was sharply increased with the pH value varying from 2.0 to 4.0, and a slight change can be found between pH 4.0 and pH 12.0. Hence, we concluded that the CDs had a relatively stable fluorescence intensity at pH 4.0 ∼ 12.0. What's more,the fluorescence quenching was observed apparently when adding the same amount of Cr(VI) to the CDs solution under pH 7.0 ∼ 12.0, and an obvious fluorescence recovery was observed when AA was added into the CDs/Cr(VI) solution under pH 7.0 ∼ 8.0. Considering the stability of Cr(VI) and AA in the reaction system and the sensitivity of the detection platform, pH 7.0 was selected for the further experiments. The fluorescence quenching time of Cr(VI) to the CDs and the recovery time of AA to the CDs/Cr(VI) system should be optimized in detail. As demonstrated in Fig. S5a, Cr(VI) can quickly quench the fluorescence of the CDs to reach the equilibrium, indicating that the reaction between the CDs and Cr(VI) is instantaneous. Meanwhile, when AA was added into the CDs/Cr(VI) system, the fluorescence intensity tended to be stable within 12 min (Fig. S5b). Therefore, Cr(VI) was added into the CDs and tested immediately, while 12 min was chosen as the optimal response time for the detection of AA based on the CDs/Cr(VI) system in the subsequent experiments.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, different amounts of Cr(VI) were added into the CDs solution. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon appears at 467 nm in the range of 0 ∼ 300 μM Cr(VI). Moreover, the fluorescence response F0/F of the CDs to Cr(VI) shows a good linear relationship in the range of 0.88 ∼ 189.33 μM with the regression equation of F0/F = 0.02244C(Cr(VI)) + 0.8989 and a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9925, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the CDs in the absence and presence of Cr(VI), respectively. C(Cr(VI)) is the concentration of Cr(VI). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.16 μM. The selectivity and anti-interference of the CDs for Cr(VI) detection are another crucial factors for this detection platform. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, no significant fluorescence quenching can be found when most of metal ions (1.0 mM) and anions (1.0 mM) were added into the CDs solution other than Cr(VI) (1.0 mM), which may be attributed to a strong affinity and fast chelating process between Cr(VI) and carboxylate or organonitrogen groups on the surface of the CDs. Moreover, as displayed in Fig. S6a, the fluorescence quenching did not change significantly after adding metal ions and anions with a concentration of 25 mM to the detection platform, while Cr(VI) with a concentration of 0.25 mM in the mixture solution of the CDs and metal ions (25 mM) or anions (25 mM) can induce intense fluorescence quenching, demonstrating that the other ions have no distinct interference for the detection of Cr(VI) by the constructed platform. Hence, an “on-off” fluorescent platform for Cr(VI) detection was developed based on the CDs with good selectivity and high sensitivity.

AA is an outstanding reducing agent, which can reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III) or Cr(0) [48,49]. Therefore, AA can restore the fluorescence of the CDs/Cr(VI) system. On account of this fact, an “off-on” detection platform for AA detection can be constructed based on the CDs/Cr(VI) system. As depicted in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence intensity was gradually strengthened with the addition of the different concentrations of AA (0 ∼ 270 μM) into the CDs/Cr(VI) system. A good linear correlation in the range of 0.43 ∼ 25.74 μM was observed at 467 nm, and the linear equation and LOD are F − F0 = 5.2559C(AA) − 2.6010 (R2 = 0.9934) and 0.1 μM, respectively. F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of the CDs/Cr(VI) system with and without AA, respectively. C(AA) is the concentration of AA. Furthermore, the selectivity and anti-interference of the CDs/Cr(VI) system towards AA detection were evaluated in detailed. As displayed in Fig. 5d, the representative interferents including Ala, Asp, Trp, Thr, Iso, Ser, His, Asn, Lys, Gly, Leu, Glu, Cys, Tyr, DA, and H2O2 with the same concentration of AA (0.05 mM) were added into the CDs/Cr(VI) system. Except for AA, no noticeable fluorescence recovery phenomenon was found, indicating that these molecules had an inappreciable effect on the fluorescence of the CDs/Cr(VI) system. The results illustrate that the “off–on” detection platform for the detection of AA has good selectivity. The anti-interference experiment results in Fig. S6b demonstrate that the detection of AA cannot be affected by the tested interferences, demonstrating that the constructed platform for AA detection has excellent anti-interference performance. As a consequence, an “off-on” fluorescent platform for AA detection was developed based on the CDs/Cr(VI) system. Compared with some previous reports (Table S2), the “on–off–on” fluorescence platform for the detection of Cr(VI) and AA is simpler, more rapid, wider linear range, and higher sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism for Cr(VI) and AA
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence quenching mechanism mainly includes intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET), and inner filter effect (IFE) [50]. To explore the fluorescent sensing mechanism for Cr(VI), the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) and the fluorescent spectra of the CDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) (blue line) exhibits three board absorption bands at 262 nm, 354 nm, and 445 nm, and the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the CDs are 357 nm and 467 nm, respectively. Obviously, the high spectral overlap can be found between the absorption band of Cr(VI) and the excitation and emission spectra of the CDs, leading to the occurrence of IFE. Moreover, we can see from Fig. S7a that the absorption spectra around 357 nm keep rising with the continuous addition of Cr(VI), meaning that Cr(VI) preemptively absorbs the excitation light of the CDs, and then fluorescence IFE between the CDs and Cr(VI) occurred, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of the CDs by Cr(VI). Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs without and with Cr(VI) were investigated to distinguish dynamic quenching or static quenching. As displayed in Fig. 6b and Table S3, the average fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs vary slightly with the addition of Cr(VI), which confirms that the quenching mechanism of Cr(VI) in our study is a static quenching process. As a consequence, the sensing mechanism of the CDs on Cr(VI) is attributed to IFE and static quenching.

The detection mechanism of AA by the CDs/Cr(VI) system may be attributed to two aspects. One is the strong interaction between AA and Cr(VI), which weakens the interaction between Cr(VI) and the CDs and strips Cr(VI) from the surface of the CDs, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of the CDs/Cr(VI) system [52]. The other is that Cr(VI) was reduced to low state (Cr(III) or Cr(0)), preventing the effective occurrence of IFE [36,51]. The sensing mechanism of the “off-on” fluorescent system for AA can be elaborated below. As depicted in Fig. 5c and Fig. S7b, the quenched fluorescence of the CDs/Cr(VI) system was recovered in the presence of AA, and the absorption spectrum around 357 nm gradually declined with the addition of AA. The phenomenon is attributed to the reaction between the strong reducibility of AA and the powerful oxidizing property of Cr(VI) [36,51]. Under the mild reaction conditions (such as room temperature and acidic or even weakly alkaline solution), the enediol in AA was oxidized, and Cr(VI) was reduced to Cr(III). And we also notice that no IFE can be found between the CDs and Cr(III) (Fig. S7c), resulting in the reduction of IFE and the fluorescence recovery of the CDs. Therefore, the CDs and the CDs/Cr(VI) system can be constructed as an “on–off–on” switchable fluorescence detection platform for the detection of Cr(VI) and AA, respectively.

The practicability of the “on-off-on” detection platform was further evaluated by detecting real samples from different origins, and the results are presented in Tables 1 and 2
. When different concentrations (10, 50, or 110 μM) of Cr(VI) were spiked into the pretreated samples, the sample recoveries are in the range of 95.80% to 103.88% for tap water, 97.30% to 104.74% for river water, and 97.33% to 104.68% for soil sample (Table 1). And the relative standard deviations (RSDs) for five replicate measurements are between 0.25% and 4.76%. Meanwhile, when detecting AA from different fruit samples, the sample recoveries are in the range of 96.72% to 103.40% for orange, 97.34% to 103.53% for kiwi, and 98.12% to 102.76% for red pitaya, and the RSDs for five replicate measurements are between 0.83% and 3.73% (Table 2). Therefore, the fluorescence CDs are promising as an efficient platform for the detection of Cr(VI) and AA in the actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application potential of the CDs in other areas
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, in order to prevent the fluorescence of the solid-state CDs from quenching due to aggregation, self-absorption, and π-π interactions, PVA is chosen as an ideal matrix for its polymer skeleton, which can stabilize emission state and prevent the aggregation of the CDs [34]. As depicted in Fig. 7 a, the obtained film can emit bright blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm), and the “on–off-on” effect of the CDs by adding Cr(VI) or AA respectively were verified by the fluorescent film. “Yin” and “Yang” of the Tai Chi diagram are taken on a piece of filter paper before and after adding Cr(VI) into the CDs solution under daylight and UV light (Fig. 7b), manifesting that the prepared CDs have potential application in the information security and anti-counterfeiting.

In the present study, the water-soluble CDs with strong blue fluorescence were synthesized with L-tartaric acid, citric acid, and urea by a green, facile and economic microwave-assisted pyrolysis method. Based on the phenomenon that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was significantly quenched in the presence of Cr(VI) and obviously restored when AA being introduced, an “on-off-on” switchable strategy for the detection of Cr(VI) and AA was developed, which had good sensitivity with the LODs of 0.16 μM Cr(VI) and 0.1 μM AA, respectively. Furthermore, the proposed strategy was successfully used for the detection of Cr(VI) in some actual water samples and AA in various fruit samples. More importantly, the CDs showed potential applications in the fluorescent film, information security, and anti-counterfeiting. To some extent, this work enriches the preparation strategy of the CDs and expands the application range of the CDs.

Yanchun Jia: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Suling Wu: Investigation, Methodology. Zhengyi Duan: Formal analysis. Shengmei Song: Software. Shaomin Shuang: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Xiaojuan Gong: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Chuan Dong: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Central Guidance Fund for Science and Technology Development (YDZJSX2021A004) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087) were thanked for supporting this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121343.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
